Off Calendar Dec-20-2018 Off Calendar

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Plaintiff And Cross-Defendant Michael P. Gutas Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Dec-06-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Jessica Riggin, Esq., Rukin, Hyland & Riggin for Plaintiff, 415-421-1800;
Erik Weiss, Esq., Murphy Pearson Bradley & Feeney for Defendant 415-788-1900;

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Plaintiff And Cross-Defendant Michael P. Guta's Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication

Plaintiff and cross-defendant Michael Guta's motion for summary judgment and alternative motion for summary adjudication on his complaint for breach of contract and common counts and Casey Securities, LLC's first amended cross-complaint for rescission, breach of contract, and fraud are denied in their entirety. There are triable disputes regarding the amount of money owed to Mr. Guta and whether the parties had a good faith disagreement about that sum. (Guta Dec., par. 12; Gasparini Dec., par. 4.) The court overrules Mr. Guta's objection to the Mr. Gasparini's declaration. The recital in the employment contract is not dispositive and the recital was merely an approximation. There is also a triable dispute whether Mr. Guta agreed to release the disputed debt in exchange for the employment contract and whether CSL reasonably relied on Mr. Guta's representations. (Gasparini Dec., pars. 8-11.) Wihelm v. Pray, Price, Williams & Russell (1986) 186 Cal.App.3d 1323, 1332 is distinguishable given the client-counsel relationship between CSL and Mr. Guta. Mr. Guta fails to maintain his summary motion burden as to CSL's breach of contract cause of action. Mr. Guta argues that he communicated the only written settlement offer that he received in the Meridian litigation and CSL is speculating about the uncommunicated offer. Mr. Gasparini's deposition does not show that CSL is unable to establish damages within the meaning of CCP 437c and Mr. Guta admitted that he failed to convey the offer. (Gasparini Depo., 191:23-192:1.)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: W. Trupek, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com., Reported. =(302/HK)

Plaintiff Michael Guta'S Motion To Compel Production Of Docs Responsive To Req For Production Set One
Off Calendar May-21-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Michael P. Guta, Tel. No. 1-415-269-8496, Plaintiff appearing in pro per;
Atty. Erik P. Weiss of Murphy Pearson Bradley & Feeney, Tel. No. 1-415-788-1900
appearing for the Defendant.

RULING - A R G U E D; Plaintiff Michael Guta's motion to compel production of documents responsive to requests for production set one is granted in part. Defendant Casey Securities, LLC is required to produce all documents responsive to requests 1 (as modified by Mr. Guta at the hearing before Judge Pro Tem Tom Cohen to exclude all documents sent after April 24, 2017 and to be limited to eight agents specified by Mr. Guta), 8 and 10-16 no later than May 29, 2018 with a verification stating that all such documents have been produced. CSL is not required to produce any documents responsive to requests 3-5. Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR #8600 =302/hk

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT / Notice Of Motion For Summary Judgment Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Mar-27-2018 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Michael Guta, Esq., T:415.269.8406, cross defendant/moving party appearing on his own behalf;
Erik P. Weiss, Esq. of Murphy Pearson Bradley & Feeney, T:415.788.1900, for cross complainant/opposing party.

RULING: The tentative ruling was contested and argued. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopts the tentative ruling, modified as follows:

CROSS DEFENDANT MICHAEL GUTA'S Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative Summary Adjudication of all three causes of action in the first amended cross-complaint filed by cross-complainant Casey Securities, LLC is DENIED in its entirety. As to the first cause of action for rescission, there is a triable dispute whether CSL, aware of concerns raised by independent counsel, sought and received assurances from Mr. Guta on which it could justifiably rely that the employment agreement subsumed Mr. Guta's claim for unpaid wages. (Fitzpatrick Dec. 4; Gasparani Dec. 10). There is also a triable issue whether the amount owed was genuinely disputed. (Gaspariani Dec. 4-6 and Fizpatrick Dec. 3) As to the second cause of action for breach of contract, there is a triable issue whether Mr. Guta breached the contract by violating his fiduciary duty to communicate important developments in the litigation by failing to communicate settlement offers in the Meridian matter and whether Mr. Guta had a conflict of interest by continuing to bill hourly for the Meridian litigation and purposefully protracting it in violation of his duty of loyalty. (Gaspariani Dec. 12-13) As to the third cause of action for fraud, there is a triable dispute whether CSL could justifiably rely on Mr. Gupta's representaions and whether there was a bona fide dispute over Mr. Guta's wage claim.

ORDER SIGNED.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: G. Gonzales; Reporter: Laura Reding, CSR 711, email: lreding@sftc.org.

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatories, Set Three; And Requests For Production, Set Five
Off Calendar Mar-27-2018 Continued To Apr-05-18 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 302
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Erik P. Weiss, Esq. of Murphy Pearson Bradley & Feeney, T:415.788.1900, for defendant/moving party;
Michael Guta, Esq., T:415.269.8406, plaintiff appearing on his own behalf.

RULING: The parties discussed the court's tentative ruling which the Court adopted after the hearing, as follows:

DEFENDANT CASEY SECURITIES LLC'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatories, Set Three; And Requests For Production, Set Five is granted in part. The hearing is CONTINUED to April 5, 2018 to give the parties an opportunity to agree on a procedure which provides directly relevant information to CSL while protecting the privacy of plaintiff Michael Guta. No later than April 2, 2018 the parties must meet and confer in an effort to agree on a neutral third party without any prior connections to either side, to be paid by CSL, who will be tasked with reviewing Mr. Guta's Gmail account and producing to CSL only those documents from that account that relate to the issues in this case. As long as the parties engage in meet and confer efforts by April 2, they may agree on a procedure different from a neutral third party review procedure. If the parties are able to reach an agreement, they should memorialize their agreement in a proposed stipulated order and, if submitted to the court prior to the April 5 hearing, that hearing will be taken off calendar.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: G. Gonzales; Reporter: Laura Reding, CSR 711, email: lreding@sftc.org.

Ntc Of Mtn For Judgment On The Pleadings
Off Calendar Nov-29-2017 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Michael P. Guta, Tel. 415.269.8696, plaintiff and moving party appearing on his own behalf;
Erik P. Weiss, Esq. of Murphy Pearson Bradley & Feeney, Tel. 415.788.1900, for defendant and opposing party.

RULING: Defendant's counsel objected to a hearing being held in this matter as plaintiff did not provide notice that the tentative rulng is contested. Therefore, the Court did not allow further argument and adopted its tentative ruling as follows: PLAINTIFF MICHAEL GUTA'S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings is DENIED. First, while the fraud claim refers to Guta's wages, the amended cross-complaint does not limit the damages to Guta's wages. (See Amended Cross-Complaint, 6:7-10; 6:14-15.) Second, the reasoning of Finnegan v. Schrader (2001) 91 Cal.App.4th 572, 584 applies to this action. The Labor Code is not violated when an employee is forced to disgorge wages procured by fraud. An employer can obtain wages where it is empowered to do so under state law. (Labor 224.) Civil Code sec. 1709 is such a law. It provides that "[o]ne who willfully deceives another with intent to induce him to alter his position to his injury or risk, is liable for any damages which he thereby suffers." Mr. Guta's reading of Labor Code sec. 221 would create a license to commit fraud in the employment context. The court denies Casey Securities, LLC's request for an OSC re CCP 128.7.

ORDER SIGNED. Judge: Newton Lam; Clerk: G. Gonzales; Reporter: Reyes F. Hunter, CSR#6576, e-mail: rhunter@sftc.org.

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Sep-07-2017 Set For Trial On Jun-18-18 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-19-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Michael Guta (415-269-8496) Plaintiff in Pro Per.
Erik Weiss, Esq. (415-788-1900) of Murphy Pearson Bradley & Feeney for Defendant.

RULING - ARGUED. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

CROSS DEFENDANT MICHAEL GUTA'S DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT OF CASEY SECURITIES, LLC, as to the third cause of action for fraud in the first amended cross-complaint of Casey Securities, LLC is OVERRULED. Mr. Guta has 15 days to answer. Mr. Guta fails to show that the employment contract is a release proscribed by Labor Code 206.5. Chindarah v. Pick Up Stix, Inc. (2009) 171 Cal.App.4th 796, 802 provides that section 206.5 prohibits the release of wages due unless paid in full. Chindarah explained that "wages are not 'due' if there is a good faith dispute as to whether they are owed." (Id.) Page 1 of the parties' agreement states that "approximately $400,000 of employees' [sic] wages remain unpaid" and "employer wishes to reduce the uncertainty of the amount of compensation to be paid to employee." This language indicates that the parties had a bona fide dispute regarding what was due and the wages. Paragraph 5 of the first amended cross-complaint also suggests that the parties had a dispute over what Mr. Guta was owed. (First Amended Cross-complaint 3:4-6.) The court cannot resolve the factual issue of whether the parties had a dispute regarding the amount owed to Mr. Guta on a demurrer. Mr. Guta's citation to Guz v. Bechtel National Inc. (2000) 24 Cal.4th 317, 352 is misplaced. CSL is not suing for tort damages based on a bad faith breach of contract. Rather, CSL alleges that it was duped into entering in the employment contract based on Mr. Guta's misrepresentations. Order signed. Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR 711, ckaren@sftc.org =(302/HK)
